questions.a: Encryption where the same secret key is used to encrypt and decrypt.
A widely used symmetric block cipher.
A value used once to make encryption unique even with the same key.
An initialization vector used to randomize some encryption modes.
Integrity and authenticity for messages using a shared secret.
It leaks patterns because identical plaintext blocks encrypt to identical ciphertext blocks.

questions.q: Why must nonces be unique in GCM?
questions.a: Nonce reuse can destroy confidentiality and integrity guarantees.

questions.q: Why do padding schemes exist in block ciphers?
questions.a: Because block ciphers operate on fixed-size blocks, but plaintext lengths vary.
It is fast in software and provides authenticated encryption.

questions.q: Why do you use a KDF for passwords?
questions.a: To slow down guessing and derive a suitable key from a low-entropy secret.

questions.q: Why is bcrypt stronger for password storage than raw SHA-256?
questions.a: It is intentionally slow and tunable, which raises brute-force cost.

questions.q: When should you choose AES-GCM over CBC?
questions.a: When you want authenticated encryption with modern safety guarantees.

questions.q: When is ChaCha20-Poly1305 a better choice?
questions.a: When software performance or mobile/embedded environments matter.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with encrypting data at rest only?
questions.a: It protects stored data, but data can still leak in memory or in transit.
It limits exposure if a key is leaked or long-lived cryptography becomes risky.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with stronger password hashing settings?
questions.a: Better brute-force resistance, but higher login latency and CPU cost.

questions.q: Why is nonce management often the hardest part of symmetric crypto systems?
questions.a: Because the algorithm can be sound while reuse or state bugs break security.
